coredata.dc:title: Theoretical and experimental studies of a novel electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer and GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lihua
He, Lihua
Wang, Qi
Tang, Qianlin
Liu, Fei
coredata.dc:description: A new molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor was prepared applying one-step electro-copolymerization of 3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) and methacrylic acid (MAA) in the presence of sulfadimidine as a template molecule on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) which modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs). A computational approach was employed to screen the suitable functional monomer, and the simulation data suggested that EDOT and MAA as bifunctional monomers exhibited a preferable capability to recognize the target molecule compared to other monomers. The MIP material was characterized with scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The performance of MIP sensor was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The results indicated that the electro-copolymerization of EDOT and MAA provides large amounts of functional groups for the binding of the template molecules, which significantly enhances the sensitivity of the MIP sensor. The calibration curve demonstrated linearity over a concentration range of 0.1 nM-0.1 mM with a detection limit of 0.023 nM. This study provides complete and valuable information on the rational design, preparation, and application of sulfadimidine molecularly imprinted polymers using advanced materials and new bi-functional monomers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted electro-synthesized copolymer
Rational MIP design
Electrochemical sensors
Bi-functional monomer
Graphene quantum dots
Pt nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Softwares, instruments, and electrochemical experiments conditions
Investigation of the π-π interaction and molecular docking
Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research described in this paper was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51702250 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Models of complexes formed by functional monomers and SM2.
π-π interaction and hydrogen bonds of the complexes in different molar ratio.
TEM images of GQDs-PtNPs with low (a) and high (b) magnification; SEM images of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE before (c) and after (d) removal of SM2.
Cyclic voltammograms of 10 m M PBS (pH 6.5) containing a) 0.5 mM EDOT, b) 0.17 mM MAA, and c) 0.5 mM EDOT and 0.17 mM MAA from 1th to 10th cycle. Scan rate: 50 mVs−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.5) at different electrodes. (a) GQDs/GCE, (b) PtNPs/GCE, (c) GQDs-PtNPs/GCE, (d) MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE after template removal, (e) NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE.

Electrochemical impedance spectra of different modified-GCEs in phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.5) containing 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl: (a) GQDs/GCE, (b) PtNPs/GCE, (c) GQDs-PtNPs/GCE, (d) MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (after template removal), (e) NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE.

(a) DPV responses of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor for different concentration of SM2. From bottom to top, the concentrations of SM2 are 0.1 nM, 1 nM, 10 nM, 25 nM, 50 nM, 75 nM, 100 nM, 500 nM, 5 µM, 10 µM, 25 µM, 50 µM, 75 µM, and 100 µM. (b) The calibration curve corresponding to SM2 in the concentration range from 0.1 nM to 100 nM. (c) The calibration curve corresponding to SM2 in the concentration range from 0.1 µM to 0.1 mM.
Schematic diagram of the MIP sensor to detect SM2.
Binding energiesΔE (kJ/mol) for the template-functional monomer complexes in 1:1 M ratio.
The π-π interaction and hydrogen bonds parameters.
Comparison of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor with other SM2 sensors.

row.entry: MIP on SiO2-coated CdTe QDs sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination and recovery results of SM2 using milk and pork sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Theoretical and experimental studies of a novel electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer and GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, Xidian University, 710126 Xi’an, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, Xidian University, 710126 Xi’an, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor was prepared applying one-step electro-copolymerization of 3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) and methacrylic acid (MAA) in the presence of sulfadimidine as a template molecule on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) which modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs). A computational approach was employed to screen the suitable functional monomer, and the simulation data suggested that EDOT and MAA as bifunctional monomers exhibited a preferable capability to recognize the target molecule compared to other monomers. The MIP material was characterized with scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The performance of MIP sensor was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The results indicated that the electro-copolymerization of EDOT and MAA provides large amounts of functional groups for the binding of the template molecules, which significantly enhances the sensitivity of the MIP sensor. The calibration curve demonstrated linearity over a concentration range of 0.1 nM-0.1 mM with a detection limit of 0.023 nM. This study provides complete and valuable information on the rational design, preparation, and application of sulfadimidine molecularly imprinted polymers using advanced materials and new bi-functional monomers.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfadimidine (SM2), one of the most common sulfonamide antibacterial, which is widely used in human and veterinary medicine for effective treatment and prevention of diseases [1]. Its extensive use and high rate of pharmaceutical consumption can lead to the appearance of residues in water via the effluents and products of animal origin. The residues are dangerous as they can cause hypersensitive allergic reactions, drug-resistance problems in human and, even carcinogenic effects [2,3]. Some scientists have confirmed that the sulfonamide can cause indeed damage to human tissue, of which approximately 95% are caused by sulfadimidine [4]. The European Union and Chinese government have already set a maximum residue limits in foods of 100 ng/g [5,6]. For food safety and environmental control, the development of a high specific, sensitive, accurate and cost method to determine the residue of sulfadimidine in foods and water samples is highly valuable.

Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) and fluorescence detection are the most commonly used analytical methods for detection of SM2. The ELISAs method is sensitive and specific, but requires complicated operational process and a laborious labeling process. At the same time, the selection of a certain antibody is a time-consuming procedure [7]. In the case of fluorescence detection, detection is non-specific and the resolution is poor [8]. On the contrary, electro-chemical methods serve as an attractive alternative to these techniques as they have various advantages like high sensitivity, simplicity of instrumentation, low cost, good stability, easy operation and portability [9–11]. Among various electrochemical methods, electrochemical sensors are easily integrated and miniaturized, and can be made into household sensors, which are truly convenient for people's lives. However, electrochemical sensors of SM2 were rarely reported.

Recently, the development of molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) electro-chemical sensor has attracted wide attention because MIPs are one of the most ideal material as the molecule recognition element in the construction of high-performance electrochemical sensors [12–15]. MIPs are synthetic materials which can be prepared by copolymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers around the template molecules to produce a network polymer. MIPs possess many attractive features including good selectivity, high chemical stability, easy preparation and low cost. However, reports on the preparation of MIPs based electrochemical sensor to examine SM2 are limited. Recently, Wei et al. [16] developed a SM2 electrochemical sensor based on MIPs/NiCo2O4 nanoneedle arrays on three-dimensional graphene electrode with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.169 ng/mL. However, the interactions between template molecule and functional monomer were ignored in this report. The tedious workload on the development of this sensor was inevitable. The recent study showed that the MIPs-analyte interaction energy and the shape of the cavities in MIPs were responsible for the MIPs’ selectivity and affinity [17]. Hence, it is still necessary to develop cost-effective approaches to solve the tedious and difficult tasks in the preparation of SM2 MIPs materials.

This research aims to enhance the quality of SM2 MIP materials from the synthesis step and reduce the time, number of experiments and cost. For those reasons, the computer molecular modeling methods have been successfully applied in this study. It is well known that density functional theory (DFT) in quantum chemical calculations is a reliable method for studying the ligand-receptor interactions. However, the dispersion forces (such as van der Waals interactions), which affect the arrangement of an organic ligand towards a target molecule (template) are not accounted by the commonly used DFT methods. In molecularly imprinted systems, dispersive forces are critical for studying the interaction between functional monomers and template molecules. Dispersion-corrected DFT (DFT-D) method that includes the following correction methods, such as TS, Grimme and OBS, provides significant improvements in accuracy through reasonable calculations [18]. To help design the SM2 MIPs rationally, DFT-D method on theoretical study of structural optimization, interaction energies calculations and solvent effects between SM2 and functional monomers was investigated in this work.

Sensitivity is another important factor for constructing MIP electrochemical sensors. Detection sensitivity is not only based on the amount of effective imprinted sites on the surface of the MIP sensor, but also governed by the functional materials in the modified electrode. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a kind of zero dimensional materials, have garnered tremendous attention in the past few years. GQDs have a series of amazing advantages, such as remarkable electrical conductivity, excellent physical and chemical properties associated with quantum-confinement and edge effects, good optical properties and low toxicity [19,20]. The GQDs can be modified via coating or deposition methods to fabricate electrochemical sensors, for instance, combined with metal nanoparticles and organic polymers [21,22]. Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs) are an excellent electro-catalyst material and have been widely used for electrochemical sensors. It has been reported that incorporation of GQDs with PtNPs could provide several merits, including promoting electro-catalytic activity of Pt NPs, preserving catalytic stability of PtNPs, and achieving cooperative synergy [23].

In this paper, a novel MIPs using EDOT and MAA as bi-functional monomers were prepared by combining the computational simulation and surface imprinted technique, and were successfully applied as a special electrochemical sensor for the determination of SM2. A DFT-D based computational approach was used to select a functional monomer for the rational design of MIPs. Further, the MIPs formation mechanism was studied by computer simulation of molecular docking. After that, the MIP electrochemical sensor using GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposites as the carrier and SM2 as the template molecule was prepared under the guidance of the calculated results. The MIP based on the bi-functional monomers showed outstanding binding capacity and low detection limit (LOD) for SM2, as predicted by the DFT-D quantum chemistry calculations. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, DFT-D quantum chemistry calculations was applied for the evaluation of the thermo-dynamic properties of an improved SM2 electrochemical sensor.

Sulfadimidine (C12H14N4O2S), 3, 4-ethoxylene dioxy thiophene (C6H6O2S), methacrylic acid (C4H6O2), hydrazine, acrylamide and chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Co (USA). Methanol (CH3OH), hydrochloricacid (HCl), citric acid (C6H8O7), potassium ferricyanide (K3 [Fe(CN)6]), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4·7H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate anhydrous (NaH2PO4), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), and high-purity graphite powder were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals used were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with double distilled water. Phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 6.5) was used as a supporting electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Softwares, instruments, and electrochemical experiments conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Computer simulation was operated through Material Studio (DMol3, v8.0, Accelrys, USA) using a workstation with Intel (R) Core (TM) i3-3120 M (2.50 GHz), RAM 4 GB and Window 8 (x64). The GGA function PBE using the TS dispersion correction was used for geometric optimization of the monomer and SM2. The MIP-GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposite was analyzed using a JSM-6390A scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) instrument (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo). Electrochemical experiments were carried out on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation at room temperature with a conventional three-electrode system, which includes a SCE as the reference electrode, a Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode, and the modified GCE as the working electrode.

In this work, material studio 8.0 software was used to study the molecular imprinting systems at atomic level. Density functional theory (DFT) in quantum chemical calculations is used for studying the ligand-receptor interactions. However, in recent years, one disadvantage that has received much attention is that most common DFTs do not accurately describe dispersion bonding, which is the weakest of non-covalent interactions [24,25]. Dispersion affects the interaction between the template and the functional monomer to a certain extent, resulting in systematic errors such as inaccurate molecular geometry [17]. Therefore, the GGA functional PBE using the TS dispersion correction was used for geometric optimization of the sulfadimidine and functional monomer [26]. At the same time, the influence of solvent water on the molecular imprinting system is studied, so that the results of the calculation simulation are closer to the experimental values. The TNP basis set was used in the calculation process. This functional is more suitable for studying the weak interactions between molecules than other functional. The computational results were used to study the recognition mechanism of MIP by discussing several parameters between the bifunctional monomers and SM2.

As starting materials, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite powder by Hummers method [27]. GQDs were prepared from GO using a reported method [28]. Briefly, 40 mL of H2O2 (30%) and 10 mL of ammonia (25–28%) were added to 5 mL of GO stock solution. The resulting mixture reacted at 80 °C with vigorous stirring for 24 h. After rotary evaporation at 65 °C to remove unreacted H2O2, ammonia and water, ethanol was used as the solvent to precipitate and wash the final GQD product. Finally, GQD was purified 3 times with a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da).

The GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposites were prepared by CO reduction of Pt (IV) precursors in the presence of GQDs [29]. In a typical experiment, 0.1 mmol of H2PtCl6·6H2O was first dissolved in 5 mL of H2O in a 25 mL glass vial. 20 mg of the GQDs was then added to the solution at a mass ratio of 1:1, and the mixture was bubbled with CO for 30 min under magnetic stirring. The vial was then sealed and under stirring overnight. The purified product were collected by centrifugation and rinsed with water.

The fabrication of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor for SM2 is shown in Scheme 1 . The GQDs-PtNPs suspension (10 μL) was carefully cast on the cleaned glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface and dried for 3–4 h at room temperature. The GQDs-PtNPs modified GCE was immersed into phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 6.5) containing monomer of EDOT (0.5 mM) and MAA (0.17 mM) and the template of SM2 (0.17 mM). The electro-polymerization was conducted via cyclic voltammetry by 15 consecutive cyclic scans in the potential range of 0.2 V and 1.4 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. After the electro-polymerization, the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE was obtained by rinsing the modified electrode in methanol – acetic acid (90:10, v/v, 30 mL) for 15 min to remove SM2 entrapped in the polymer film. A non-imprinted GCE (NIP- GQDs-PtNPs/GCE) used as the control electrode was also prepared under the same conditions but without the addition of SM2.

To study the selectivity of MIP, computer simulation of the interaction between the template and the functional monomer was performed, which would be a significant indication of the molecular imprinting ability and responsible for the selectivity of SM2. The structure of template, functional monomers and monomer-template complexes are optimized with DFT-D quantum chemistry calculations. The COSMO continuum model, for the simulation of the dielectric constant of the solvent water was used in the synthesis environment. Subsequently, a bigger TNP basis set was set. Then the 18 most stable candidates for each case were obtained, as shown in Fig. 1 . The lowest energy configuration was selected as the optimal structure. Finally, the binding energy can be calculated by the following formula:
(1)
Δ E =
E
complex
-
E
template
- - n
E
monomer
Ecomplex, Etemplate, and Emonomer are total binding energy of the complex, binding energy of the template, and binding energy of the monomer, respectively [30,31].

To find more suitable functional units, EDOT, MAA, methacrylamide (MAM), methyl methacrylate (MMA), pyrrole (Py), thiophene (Th) were studied as functional monomers and compared using DFT-D quantum chemistry calculations. It is well known that functional monomers and template can form different configurations due to different positions of action. As can be seen from Table1 and Fig. 1, there may be 6 types of combinations between sulfadimidine and EDOT. As shown in Table 1, the SM2-MAA and SM2-EDOT composites have relatively low binding energies of −45.5435 and −48.9254 kJ/mol, respectively. Therefore, MAA and EDOT are selected as bi-functional monomers. It can be seen from Fig. 1 the templates are effectively embedded into the internal composite of the bifunctional monomers complex and form a complete molecularly imprinted polymer system. The investigation lays a solid theoretical foundation for the molecular recognition of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the π-π interaction and molecular docking
ce:section.ce:para: In modern quantum-chemical calculations, stacking interactions of π-π is a very useful force for self-assembly system such as bifunctional monomer and template. Therefore, high-level quantum chemical investigations are applied to analysis the π-π interaction of the complexes [32]. In order to study the nature of the system's recognition mechanism, the distance of face-to-face (between the two centers of the conjugate rings (Z, Å)), edge-to-face (between the hydrogen atom on the ring and the center of the other ring (l, Å)), and offset (two planes (d, Å)) are calculated [33,34]. As can be seen in Table 2 , the imprinting molar ratios among SM2, MAA and EDOT were 1:1:1, 1:2:1, 1:3:1, 1:1:2, 1:2:2, 1:1:3, respectively. At different molar ratios, EDOT was anchored with SM2 through π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions [35,36]. EDOT and SM2 can form offset type (3.413 Å–4.029 Å), edge-to-face type (2.595 Å) and face-to-face type (3.593 Å–3.640 Å) by π-π interactions within the effective range. The experimental data and computational simulations indicated that π-π stacking interaction was one of the important factors for SM2 adsorption.

Furthermore, the appropriate molar ratio of SM2-MAA-EDOT is critical for the formation of MIP because the strongest non-covalent interactions can be obtained with the appropriate molar ratio of the template and bifunctional monomers. The best molar ratios were selected based on ΔE. As can be seen in Fig. 2 and Table 2, when methacrylic acid is present, hydrogen bonds are formed. The bond lengths are in the range of 1.681–1.947 Å, which is in the range with the values of hydrogen bond distance [37]. So methacrylic acid was used as one of the bifunctional monomers. A higher binding energy suggests that the SM2-MAA-EDOT combined more closely. And the binding energy of −155.378 kJ/mol (1:1:3) was larger than other complexes with different molar ratios (1:1:1, 1:2:1, 1:3:1, 1:1:2, 1:2:2). Therefore, the appropriate molar ratio of SM2-MAA-EDOT is 1:1:3.

As can be seen from Fig. 2, the molecular docking between the template and the bifunctional monomers is carried out in offset type and edge-to-face type manners. Therefore, there is strong interaction between the template and the bifunctional monomers, which implies that the MIP membrane has good stability. The optimized conformations of the template SM2 and the two functional monomers are shown in the above study. When the molar ratio was 1:1:3 (SM2:MAA:EDOT), the rebinding sites of the complexes were observed to be stronger and more efficient. The number of binding sites in the MIP were 4. It can be seen that there are unsaturated ring groups and N–H groups in SM2 molecule. The H atom (in N–H groups) of SM2 can act with O atom in methacrylic acid and form 1 binding sites. Meanwhile, the SM2-MAA-EDOT (1:1:3) complexes can form 1 edge-to-face type π-π interaction, 1 offset type π-π interaction and 1 face-to-face type π-π interaction within the effective range. These results indicated that there are 1 hydrogen bonding site and 3 different kinds of π-π interaction in the complexes.

Most analysis of the solvation effect about the MIP is based on organic phases, which greatly limited the use of MIP in the selective analysis of targets in aqueous samples, such as biological fluids. For example, Mariusz Marc compared the effects of acetonitrile and tetrahydrofuran in their research [38]. Wu studied the effects of five solvents in the calculation system, which are all organic solvent [39]. In this paper, MIP is formed in water-rich systems. Therefore, the solvent water was considered into the calculation system for the first time. The energy data in this article will be closer to the real value than the research in the vacuum environment. More important, it provides corresponding references for the simulation of the aqueous phase system. It has more practical significance for the development of MIP and can meet the needs of practical applications.

TEM and SEM were used to observe the morphologies of GQDs-PtNPs and the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs composite as shown in Fig. 3 . The typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs-PtNPs (Fig. 3a) exhibits that as-prepared GQDs-PtNPs are well-dispersed and have a relatively narrow lateral size distribution ranging from 3.5 nm to 5.5 nm (Fig. S1). Furthermore, from high-resolution TEM studies in Fig. 3b, lattice fringes can be identified with a spacing of 0.23 nm, which is in good agreement with the (111) crystalline planes of fcc Pt [40]. In Fig. 3b, GQDs were difficult to be identified because of their low electron density and poor crystallinity [41]. In MIP-adduct coating on GQDs-PtNPs surface, a compact and rough surface was found due to the direct surface imprinting (Fig. 3c). This rough surface of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs composite provided large specific surface area for the recognition and detection of SM2. Interestingly, upon SM2 templates retrieval, the surface of MIPs became rougher and more cavities released (Fig. 3d), indicating that MIP sensor was prepared successfully. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE before extraction and after extraction is provided in the Supplementary materials (Fig. S2), which also proves the successful preparation of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE.

The chemical composition of the obtained MIP/GQDs-PtNPs composite was determined using EDX analysis (Fig. 4 ). The EDX spectrum shows that the elements C, N, O, Pt and S were detected in the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs nanocomposite film modified on the ITO electrode.

Fig. 5 shows the process of EDOT and MAA electro-polymerization on GCE surface. For the electro-polymerization of EDOT, the redox wave current increased with the continuous CV scans, which implied that the amount of PEDOT deposited on the electrode was increasing (Fig. 5a). The redox peaks were attributed to the doping/de-doping processes of PEDOT films formed in CV scans [42]. For the electro-polymerization of MAA, the onset of the reduction started at −0.5 V and moved to lower potentials up to −0.8 V with increasing cycles (Fig. 5b). In the solution containing 0.5 mM EDOT and 0.17 mM MAA the CVs showed the above electrochemical responses (Fig. 5c). Upon the continuous scans, it produced a polymer film with the formation of new conjugated species. These experimental results indicated that the copolymerization film of EDOT and MAA was prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Typical CVs of the different modified electrodes were performed in stepwise fabrication process of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE with 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe solution at 50 mV s−1 from −0.2 to 0.6 V. CV curves in Fig. 6 revealed that the GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve c) significantly improved the redox peak currents and reduced peak separation of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− when compared with GQDs/GCE (curve a) and PtNPs/GCE (curve b) due to the increment of electrodes surface area by GQDs and high electro-catalytic activity of PtNPs. Furthermore, the redox peak MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve d) was strongest because the numerous recognition cavities in the polymer provided access for the probe to transfer to the electrode surface. In contrast, the redox peak of NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve e) was much weaker than that of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve d) related to the lack of active imprinting sites on the electrode surface.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is widely used to characterize the charge carrier transfer and the surface features of the modified electrode. The diameter of semicircle in the Nyquist plot represents a faradaic resistance that reflects charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrodes and Warburg impedance at low frequencies represents the frequency dependent diffusion of ions within the electrode [43]. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist impedance diagrams, which are obtained on the GQDs/GCE (b), PtNPs/GCE (c), GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (a), MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE after template removal (d) and NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (e). The semicircular diameter of the GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve c) was evidently lower than that of the GQDs/GCE (a), PtNPs/GCE (b). The decrease in Rct of GQDs-PtNPs/GCE can be attributed to the greater conductive nature of GQDs and PtNPs. When the templates were removed from the MIP layer, the Rct value of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE was considerably decreased (curve d). It can be concluded that removal of templates from the electrode surface permits electrons to be transferred through the formed holes at the electrode, resulting in enhancement of the charge transfer rate. The Rct of NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE (curve e) was much higher than that of MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE, because the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE had recognition cavities and offered less resistance to the redox probe.

To get an excellent performance of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor, pH of the phosphate buffer solution, the electro-polymerization conditions, the amount of template, monomer, elution time and incubation time were optimized for the determination of SM2. The details are provided in the Supplementary materials.

Under optimal experimental conditions, DPV was used to quantitatively detect SM2 in solutions. Fig. 8 a exhibited the DPV responses of the SM2 on the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor. The oxidation potential was 0.22 V and the peak current (i) was linearly related with the concentration of SM2 in the range of 0.1 nM to 0.1 mM. In the concentration range of 0.1 nM to 100 nM, the first equation was described as i (μA) = 0.6796c (nM) + 7.9362 in Fig. 8b. In the concentration range of 0.5 μM to 100 μM, the second equation was described as i (μA) = 0.997c (μM) + 82.469 in Fig. 8c. Furthermore, the detection limit was calculated as 0.023 nM (S/N = 3), which was much lower than the standard value for food quality of China, and lower than previous results by electrochemical sensors [16,44]. Also, sensitivity of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor was found to be 161.8 µA⋅µM−1⋅cm−2 based on slope of the calibration curve and the electrode surface area. The performance of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor had also been compared with other SM2 sensors [45,46], as shown in Table 3 . Overall, the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor is better in terms of lower detection limit, selectivity, stable, ease of operation and cost.

The stability of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor was tested for more than 15 days. The sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and used for the determination of SM2 with DPV after every 2 days in phosphate buffer solution at pH 6.5 with a fixed SM2 concentration of 1 μM. The sensor kept 92.5% of its original current of SM2 with a RSD of 4.1%. This stability indicates that the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE can be used for a relatively long time to detect SM2. Five sensors, prepared under the same conditions, were used to detect 1 μM of SM2 with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.3%. In addition, the reproducibility of five different batches of modified electrodes prepared with the same procedure was also investigated by DPV. The RSD was calculated to be 3.7%, confirming good reproducibility of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensor.

To evaluate the selectivity of the imprinted sensor, some structural analogues such as sulfamethoxazole (SMZ) and sulfadiazine (SDZ) and sulfapyridine (SPD) were selected as interferences to research the specific selection performance of this molecularly imprinted sensor for SM2. As show in Fig. S7, the response (iP ) of SM2 at the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE was 21, 23 and 24 times more than that of SMZ, SDZ, and SPD (10 μM for each compound), respectively, indicating the non-interference of these structurally similar compounds to SM2 molecularly imprinted sensor. In additional, the current response of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE toward SM2 was nearly 27 times than that of the NIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE, indicating the sensitive recognition ability of the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE toward the templates. These results suggested that the MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensing system can well discriminate SM2 in complex samples from its analogues.

The applicability of the MIP-PDA-GQDs/GCE sensor was evaluated by analysis of milk samples and pork samples. Using spike recovery method for the determination of SM2 in sample. Prior to the detection, the samples were pretreated. Three measurements were performed at each concentration. The results are listed in Table 4 , showing that the values of recovery vary from 95.3% to 104.8%. These results indicated the prepared MIP-PDA-GQDs/GCE sensor is feasible for the determination of SM2 in real samples.

In summary, a new MIP/GQDs-PtNPs/GCE sensing system has been successfully constructed via a facile one-step electro-copolymerization method for highly sensitive detection of trace amount SM2 in food. Using DFT-D method, a comprehensive computer simulation strategy was used to combine molecular docking and the solvation effect in the screening of bifunctional monomers for the rational design of molecularly imprinted materials. The DFT-D method is used not only for studying monomer-template interactions, but also to assess the stoichiometry, π-π interaction, molecular docking and thermodynamics properties such as binding energy.

The developed sensor has two innovations. First, it is the first molecularly imprinted material prepared with EDOT and MAA as bi-functional monomers. Second, this is the first report of using computer simulation for the design of SM2 electrochemical sensor. The sensor is better in terms of higher sensitive, specific, stable, ease of operation and cost. One-step electro-copolymerization process led to a robust integration of MIP film with a transducer. Therefore, this method simplifies the preparation of MIP material and lowers the cost. This study provides a comprehensive design, preparation and application of SM2 MIP materials. The electro-copolymerization system in this study also provides a significant reference for electro-polymerization reactions and may be extended to supercapacitors and fuel cells.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research described in this paper was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51702250).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105196.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
